name: Phishing
description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems. All forms of phishing are electronically delivered social engineering. Phishing can be targeted (spearphishing) or broadcast to a wide audience.

name: Spearphishing Attachment
description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with malicious attachments to gain access. The attachment may contain exploits or payloads.

name: Spearphishing Link
description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with malicious links to gain access. Links may direct users to credential harvesting pages or drive-by download sites.

name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
description: Adversaries may exploit weaknesses in internet-facing systems to gain initial access. Common targets include web servers, databases, and VPN appliances.

name: Valid Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts as a means of gaining Initial Access, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, or Defense Evasion.

name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands. These interpreters provide a way to interact with computer systems and execute code.

name: PowerShell
description: Adversaries may abuse PowerShell for execution. PowerShell is a powerful scripting environment widely used for both legitimate administration and malicious activity.

name: Windows Command Shell
description: Adversaries may abuse cmd.exe to execute commands. The Windows command shell is the primary command-line interface on Windows systems.

name: User Execution
description: Adversaries may rely on user execution to gain access. Users may be tricked into opening malicious files or executing code.

name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
description: Adversaries may configure system settings to automatically execute programs on system boot or user logon to maintain persistence.

name: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
description: Adversaries may achieve persistence by adding programs to startup folders or registry run keys that execute on system boot or user logon.

name: Scheduled Task/Job
description: Adversaries may abuse task scheduling to execute code at system startup or on a scheduled basis for persistence or privilege escalation.

name: Create Account
description: Adversaries may create accounts to maintain access to victim systems. Accounts may be local, domain, or cloud-based.

name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities to elevate privileges. Exploitation may target OS kernels, drivers, or application vulnerabilities.

name: Access Token Manipulation
description: Adversaries may manipulate access tokens to escalate privileges or impersonate users. Token manipulation can bypass security controls.

name: Process Injection
description: Adversaries may inject code into processes to evade process-based defenses and escalate privileges. Common techniques include DLL injection, PE injection, and thread execution hijacking.

name: Impair Defenses
description: Adversaries may disable or tamper with security tools to avoid detection. This includes disabling antivirus, EDR, firewalls, and logging.

name: Disable or Modify Tools
description: Adversaries may disable security tools by stopping services, killing processes, or uninstalling tools.

name: Indicator Removal
description: Adversaries may delete or modify artifacts to cover their tracks, including logs, prefetch files, browser history, and event logs.

name: Clear Windows Event Logs
description: Adversaries may clear Windows Event Logs to hide traces of their activity using wevtutil, PowerShell, or direct API calls.

name: Credentials from Password Stores
description: Adversaries may extract credentials from password managers, credential vaults, and other password stores.

name: Credentials from Web Browsers
description: Adversaries may extract credentials from web browsers by reading stored passwords from browser password managers.

name: OS Credential Dumping
description: Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials from the operating system to obtain account credentials. Tools like Mimikatz are commonly used.

name: LSASS Memory
description: Adversaries may dump LSASS memory to extract credential material. Tools like procdump, Mimikatz, and direct API calls target LSASS.

name: Process Discovery
description: Adversaries may get information about running processes on a system. This helps them understand the environment and identify targets.

name: File and Directory Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate files and directories to understand the filesystem layout and locate interesting files.

name: System Information Discovery
description: Adversaries may gather system information including OS version, hostname, architecture, and installed software.

name: System Network Configuration Discovery
description: Adversaries may look for network configuration settings to understand the network layout and identify potential targets.

name: Remote Services
description: Adversaries may use valid accounts to log into remote machines using remote access protocols like RDP, SSH, WinRM, and VNC.

name: Remote Desktop Protocol
description: Adversaries may use RDP to access remote systems. RDP is a common target for brute-force attacks and credential abuse.

name: Lateral Tool Transfer
description: Adversaries may transfer tools between systems to support lateral movement. Common methods include SMB, scp, certutil, and BITSAdmin.

name: Data Staged
description: Adversaries may collect and stage data in a central location before exfiltration. This helps them organize exfiltrated data.

name: Data from Local System
description: Adversaries may search local system sources for data of interest. This includes files, databases, browser data, and local email stores.

name: Application Layer Protocol
description: Adversaries may communicate using application layer protocols to blend in with normal traffic. Common protocols include HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, and SMTP.

name: Web Protocols
description: Adversaries may use HTTP/HTTPS for C2 communication. Web traffic is commonly allowed through firewalls and blends with legitimate traffic.

name: Encrypted Channel
description: Adversaries may use encrypted C2 channels to hide the content of their communications. This includes custom encryption, SSL/TLS, and obfuscation.

name: Non-Application Layer Protocol
description: Adversaries may use non-application layer protocols like ICMP, TCP raw sockets, or UDP for C2 communication to evade detection.

name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data over different protocols than the original C2 channel, often to evade detection.

name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data over the existing C2 channel, using the same protocol and infrastructure.

name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data using web services like cloud storage, paste sites, or social media platforms.

name: Data Destruction
description: Adversaries may destroy data on target systems to disrupt availability and cause damage.

name: Data Encrypted for Impact
description: Adversaries may encrypt data on target systems to disrupt availability and demand ransom. This is characteristic of ransomware.

name: Inhibit System Recovery
description: Adversaries may delete or remove system recovery data to prevent recovery after disruption. This includes deleting Volume Shadow Copies and backups.

name: System Shutdown/Reboot
description: Adversaries may shutdown or reboot systems to disrupt access or as part of destructive malware.

name: Supply Chain Compromise
description: Adversaries may compromise third-party software or hardware to gain access to victim networks through trusted vendors.

name: Subvert Trust Controls
description: Adversaries may hijack or bypass trust mechanisms to install malicious code that appears legitimate through modified certificates or trust stores.

name: Signed Binary Proxy Execution
description: Adversaries may use signed, trusted binaries to proxy execution of malicious code. Known as living-off-the-land binaries (LOLBins).

name: Rundll32
description: Adversaries may use rundll32.exe to execute arbitrary DLLs. Rundll32 is a legitimate Windows binary often abused for code execution.

name: Mshta
description: Adversaries may use mshta.exe to execute malicious HTA files. Mshta is a legitimate utility for executing Microsoft HTML Applications.

name: Malicious File
description: Adversaries may rely on a user opening a malicious file to gain code execution. Files include Office documents, PDFs, and scripts.

name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
description: Adversaries may bypass UAC or sudo to escalate privileges using bypass techniques that abuse elevation control mechanisms.

name: Bypass User Account Control
description: Adversaries may bypass UAC to execute code with elevated privileges without triggering a UAC prompt.

name: Obfuscated Files or Information
description: Adversaries may obfuscate files or information to evade detection. Common techniques include encoding, encryption, compression, and packing.

name: Command Obfuscation
description: Adversaries may obfuscate commands during execution to evade detection using encoding, variable substitution, and string manipulation.

name: Masquerading
description: Adversaries may manipulate file names, extensions, or metadata to make malicious content appear legitimate or benign.

name: Match Legitimate Name or Location
description: Adversaries may name executables or place them in locations that match legitimate system files to evade detection.

name: Query Registry
description: Adversaries may interact with the Windows Registry to gather information about the system, configuration, and software.

name: Software Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate installed software and security tools to understand the environment and determine if any security products are present.

name: Permission Groups Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate user permissions, groups, and domain administrators to identify privileged accounts.
detection: Group enumeration commands (net group, net localgroup)

name: Account Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate user accounts to identify valid credentials, privileged accounts, and targets for lateral movement.

name: System Network Connections Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate active network connections to identify remote systems and services for lateral movement.

name: Remote System Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate remote systems to identify targets for lateral movement using tools like ping, nbtstat, and LDAP queries.

name: Proxy
description: Adversaries may use proxies to route C2 traffic through intermediate servers to obscure the source of communications.

name: Multi-hop Proxy
description: Adversaries may route C2 traffic through multiple proxy hops to make tracking more difficult.

name: Fallback Channels
description: Adversaries may use multiple C2 channels with fallback mechanisms to maintain communication if primary channels are blocked.

name: Ingress Tool Transfer
description: Adversaries may transfer tools from external systems to the victim environment to support operations.

name: Brute Force
description: Adversaries may use brute-force techniques to gain access to accounts by guessing or cracking passwords.

name: Password Spraying
description: Adversaries may attempt to log in using a single password against many accounts to avoid account lockout.

name: Use Alternate Authentication Material
description: Adversaries may use alternative authentication material like pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, or Kerberos tickets to access systems.

name: Pass the Hash
description: Adversaries may use captured NTLM hashes to authenticate without knowing the plaintext password.

name: Windows Management Instrumentation
description: Adversaries may use WMI for execution, lateral movement, discovery, and persistence. WMI provides a powerful management interface.

name: Software Deployment Tools
description: Adversaries may abuse enterprise software deployment tools to move laterally and execute code across the environment.

name: Exploitation for Client Execution
description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities in client applications to execute code. Common targets include browsers, Office, and PDF readers.

name: Data Encoding
description: Adversaries may encode data to make it harder for defenders to detect exfiltrated content or C2 traffic.

name: Email Collection
description: Adversaries may collect email data from targets to gather sensitive information or intelligence.

name: Local Email Collection
description: Adversaries may collect email data from local email clients like Outlook by accessing PST files or MAPI.

name: Archive Collected Data
description: Adversaries may compress or encrypt collected data before exfiltration to reduce size and evade detection.

name: Archive via Utility
description: Adversaries may use built-in or third-party archiving utilities like WinRAR, 7-Zip, or tar to package collected data.

name: Data from Cloud Storage
description: Adversaries may access cloud storage services (OneDrive, Google Drive, S3) to collect sensitive data.

name: Account Manipulation
description: Adversaries may manipulate accounts to maintain or escalate access by modifying credentials or permissions.

name: Additional Email Delegate Permissions
description: Adversaries may add delegate permissions to mailboxes to maintain persistent access to email data.
